title: Process Modeling Hierarchy
content: 4-level hierarchy: Level 0 Value Chain (enterprise); Level 1 Process Area (business domain e.g. Order Management); Level 2 Process/Workflow (BPMN diagram); Level 3 Activity (task detail with steps); Level 4 Procedure (step-by-step instructions). Start at Level 0 to avoid missing context.
tags: process-hierarchy value-chain process-area workflow activity procedure

title: BPMN Start Event
content: BPMN Start Event (thin circle): triggers process initiation. Types: Blank (no trigger); Message (receives message); Timer (time-based trigger); Signal (receives signal); Conditional (condition becomes true). Every process MUST have at least one start event.
tags: BPMN start-event event notation trigger circle

title: BPMN Intermediate Event
content: BPMN Intermediate Event (double circle): occurs during process flow. Types: Message (send/receive); Timer (wait/delay); Signal; Error (catch errors); Compensation. Used to model waiting states pauses or message exchanges mid-process.
tags: BPMN intermediate-event double-circle message timer signal error notation

title: BPMN End Event
content: BPMN End Event (thick circle): marks process completion. Types: Blank (normal end); Message (send message on end); Error (terminates with error); Terminate (kills all parallel tokens); Compensation. Every process flow path must end with an end event.
tags: BPMN end-event thick-circle terminate error notation

title: BPMN Task Types
content: BPMN Task is atomic work unit. Types: User Task (human performs with system support); Service Task (automated system call); Script Task (runs script/code); Manual Task (performed without system); Receive Task (waits for message); Send Task (sends message); Business Rule Task (evaluates decision table).
tags: BPMN task user-task service-task script-task manual-task activity notation

title: BPMN Sub-Process
content: BPMN Sub-Process: collapsed (rectangle with + marker showing contained process) or expanded (shows internal flow). Used to manage complexity; hide detail until needed; enable reuse across processes. Sub-processes can be event-driven or looping.
tags: BPMN sub-process collapsed expanded reuse complexity notation activity

title: BPMN Exclusive Gateway XOR
content: Exclusive Gateway (diamond with X): only ONE outgoing path taken based on condition. Used for decisions with mutually exclusive outcomes. Each outgoing sequence flow MUST have a condition label. One path should be default. Decision must be deterministic.
tags: BPMN exclusive-gateway XOR decision diamond condition routing

title: BPMN Parallel Gateway AND
content: Parallel Gateway (diamond with +): ALL outgoing paths activated simultaneously. Creates parallel branches; synchronizes parallel branches when used as join. No conditions needed on outgoing flows. Join waits for ALL incoming tokens before proceeding.
tags: BPMN parallel-gateway AND split join concurrent diamond notation

title: BPMN Inclusive Gateway OR
content: Inclusive Gateway (diamond with circle): ONE OR MORE outgoing paths taken. More flexible than XOR; less predictable than AND. Each outgoing flow has condition; join waits for all active paths. Use when multiple conditions can be true simultaneously.
tags: BPMN inclusive-gateway OR multiple-paths diamond condition notation

title: BPMN Event-Based Gateway
content: Event-Based Gateway (diamond with pentagon): routing based on WHICH event occurs first. Each outgoing flow leads to an intermediate catching event. Process takes the path of the first event received. Used for race conditions and timeouts.
tags: BPMN event-based-gateway race-condition event-first routing notation

title: BPMN Sequence Flow
content: Sequence Flow (solid arrow): represents order of activities within a pool/lane. Must connect flow objects within same pool. Cannot cross pool boundaries (use Message Flow instead). Labels should describe condition for conditional flows.
tags: BPMN sequence-flow arrow connecting-objects flow order notation

title: BPMN Message Flow
content: Message Flow (dashed arrow): represents communication between different pools/participants/organizations. Crosses pool boundaries. Shows interaction not internal flow. Key for modeling B2B processes or department handoffs.
tags: BPMN message-flow dashed-arrow pools participants communication collaboration

title: BPMN Association
content: BPMN Association (dotted line): links artifacts (data objects annotations) to flow elements. Not a flow itself; just shows relationship or annotation. Used with text annotations to add explanations to complex elements.
tags: BPMN association dotted-line artifact annotation data-object connection

title: BPMN Pool and Swimlane
content: Pool represents one participant/organization in a collaboration. Lanes within a pool represent roles departments or systems. Activities in a lane are performed by that lane's actor. Cross-lane flows represent handoffs. Each pool has its own start/end events.
tags: BPMN pool swimlane lane participant role department handoff organization

title: Swimlane Construction Rules
content: Swimlane best practices: One pool per organization/system; lanes for roles within organization; place tasks in lane of performer; use message flow for inter-pool communication; label all sequence flows at gateways; all lanes belong to one pool; avoid too many lanes (max 5-6 for readability).
tags: swimlane construction BPMN lanes roles pools readability best-practice

title: As-Is Analysis Framework
content: As-Is documentation captures: Process name/owner/scope; Trigger and output; Frequency and volume; Current metrics (cycle time throughput error rate cost satisfaction); Process steps (activity actor system duration pain points); Pain points summary with root cause; Systems and data used.
tags: as-is analysis current-state process-documentation pain-points metrics

title: To-Be Design Framework
content: To-Be design defines future state with improvements over As-Is. Elements: automation replacing manual steps; digitization of paper/email processes; optimization for speed/cost; new capabilities; elimination of waste. Gap between As-Is and To-Be drives requirements.
tags: to-be design future-state automation digitization optimization improvement

title: Gap Analysis Template
content: Gap types: AUTOMATION (manual to automated); DIGITIZATION (paper/email to system-based); OPTIMIZATION (faster/cheaper/better); NEW CAPABILITY (didn't exist before); ELIMINATION (remove unnecessary steps); INTEGRATION (connect disconnected systems). Each gap mapped from As-Is to To-Be with priority.
tags: gap-analysis types automation digitization optimization elimination integration

title: Process Specification Template
content: Process spec structure: Process Identity (ID name owner version date); Purpose; Triggers; Inputs; Outputs; Process Diagram reference; Detailed Steps (actor system description inputs actions outputs business rules exceptions SLA); Business Rules table; RACI Matrix; Process Metrics targets.
tags: process-specification template BPMN RACI business-rules steps metrics

title: RACI Matrix in Process Models
content: RACI assigns accountability per activity: R=Responsible (does the work); A=Accountable (owns outcome signs off only one per activity); C=Consulted (input before action); I=Informed (notified after action). Every activity must have exactly one A. Systems can be R but never A.
tags: RACI responsible accountable consulted informed process accountability matrix

title: Value Stream Mapping VSM
content: VSM shows end-to-end value delivery with timing. Elements per step: CT (Cycle Time = processing time); WT (Wait Time = queue/delay); %C&A (% Complete and Accurate = quality). Key metrics: Total Lead Time = sum of all CT+WT; Value-Added Time = sum of CT only; Process Efficiency = VA Time / Lead Time.
tags: VSM value-stream-mapping cycle-time wait-time lead-time process-efficiency lean

title: Process Efficiency Formula
content: Process Efficiency = Value-Added Time / Total Lead Time × 100%. Example: 60 minutes VA / 8 hours lead time = 12.5% efficiency. Industry average is 5-15%; lean processes achieve 30-60%. Low efficiency indicates large wait times and waste to eliminate.
tags: process-efficiency formula value-added lead-time lean VSM waste

title: 8 Types of Waste Muda Lean
content: 8 Muda (waste) types in Lean: Transportation (unnecessary movement of info/materials); Inventory (excess work in progress); Motion (unnecessary movement of people); Waiting (idle time); Overproduction (more than needed); Over-processing (more than required); Defects (errors requiring rework); Skills (unused talent).
tags: muda waste lean 8-wastes transportation inventory motion waiting defects TIM-WOODS

title: Value-Added vs Non-Value-Added Activities
content: Value-Added (VA): customer willing to pay; transforms product/service; done right first time. Non-Value-Added Necessary (NVA-N): required by regulation or business (compliance checks approvals). Non-Value-Added (NVA): pure waste to eliminate (waiting rework redundant approvals handoffs).
tags: value-added non-value-added waste lean VSM analysis classification

title: Process Modeling Checklist
content: Process modeling checklist: Scope (boundaries start/end process owner stakeholders); As-Is (all steps mapped actors identified systems documented pain points captured metrics baselined); Analysis (VA vs NVA bottlenecks root causes opportunities); To-Be (future state gap analysis business rules target metrics RACI); Notation (BPMN symbols correct swimlanes labeled gateways conditioned); Validation (SME walkthrough edge cases sign-off).
tags: process-modeling checklist BPMN as-is to-be validation notation scope

title: Process Metrics Baseline
content: As-Is process metrics to capture: Cycle Time (start to end); Throughput (units per period); Error/Defect Rate (%); Cost per Transaction ($); Customer Satisfaction (%); First-Time-Right Rate (%). Baseline these BEFORE designing To-Be to measure improvement. Set SMART targets for each.
tags: process-metrics baseline cycle-time throughput error-rate cost satisfaction

title: Process Modeling Levels L0 to L4
content: Level 0: Value Chain - enterprise-level Porter's model; Level 1: Process Area - functional domain (Order Management HR Finance); Level 2: Process/Workflow - swimlane diagram with BPMN; Level 3: Activity - task breakdown with steps; Level 4: Procedure - click-by-click instructions. BA works primarily at L1-L3.
tags: process-levels L0 L1 L2 L3 L4 value-chain workflow activity procedure granularity

title: Bottleneck Identification in VSM
content: Bottleneck = step where work accumulates (high WT) or has lowest throughput. Identification: map wait times between steps; find largest WT; find step with lowest %C&A (quality issue causing rework). Goldratt's Theory of Constraints: optimize the constraint first before other improvements.
tags: bottleneck wait-time VSM theory-of-constraints throughput lean process

title: As-Is Process Documentation Template
content: As-Is template fields: Process Name/Owner/Scope/Trigger/Output/Frequency/Volume; Metrics table (Cycle Time Throughput Error Rate Cost Satisfaction First-Time-Right); Steps table (Step Activity Actor System Duration Pain Points); Pain Points summary (ID Description Impact Root Cause); Systems/Data/Integrations/Manual Handoffs.
tags: as-is template documentation process steps metrics pain-points systems

title: Process Boundary Definition
content: Process boundaries define what is in-scope and out-of-scope. Start boundary = trigger event (what initiates process). End boundary = output/outcome. Scope statement: "This process starts when [trigger] and ends when [output delivered to recipient]. Excludes [out-of-scope activities]."
tags: process-boundary scope trigger output in-scope out-of-scope definition

title: Lean Six Sigma DMAIC
content: DMAIC improvement framework: Define (problem scope goals); Measure (current performance baseline); Analyze (root cause identification); Improve (solution design and pilot); Control (sustain improvements standardize). Used with Value Stream Mapping and waste elimination. Combines Lean speed with Six Sigma quality.
tags: DMAIC lean six-sigma improvement define measure analyze improve control

title: Process Exception Handling
content: Every process step may have exceptions. Exception handling pattern: "If [condition]
tags: then [alternative flow]." Document: exception type; trigger condition; responsible actor; resolution steps; escalation path; SLA for exception resolution. Missing exception handling = incomplete process model.
agents: exception-handling alternative-flow escalation BPMN process edge-cases

title: Activity Diagram vs BPMN
content: Activity Diagram (UML): good for detailed workflow within single system/component; shows object flows and partitions (swimlanes). BPMN: better for cross-organizational processes; richer event notation; explicit message flows between pools; industry standard for business processes. Use BPMN for BA work BPMN 2.0.
tags: activity-diagram UML BPMN comparison workflow notation standard

title: As-Is Documentation Checklist
content: As-Is checklist: Current process mapped with all steps; All actors/roles identified; Systems and tools documented; Pain points captured with evidence (data not opinion); Current metrics baseline established (cycle time error rate cost); Exceptions and workarounds noted; Validation by process participants.
tags: as-is checklist documentation pain-points metrics validation actors systems

title: Process Owner vs Process Actor
content: Process Owner: accountable for process performance and improvement; has authority to change process; signs off on To-Be design; monitors metrics. Process Actor: performs activities within the process; subject matter expert for their lane; provides as-is information. Identify both at project start.
tags: process-owner actor accountability RACI stakeholder roles
